SUMMARY:
We conducted semi-structured interviews with nine emergency care and hospital laboratory stakeholders with expertise and responsibility over POCT operations within hospital systems during the COVID-19 pandemic in Maryland, USA. However, there was a limited emphasis on diagnostic tests not performed in laboratories [11]**.** The task force can broaden its engagement with pertinent stakeholders such as public health experts, hospital leadership, test developers to improve the effectiveness of the federal diagnostic response during an infectious disease emergency, particularly regarding the utilization of other diagnostic tests such as POCT [17]. Dixon, B.J. Meenan Barriers to hospital-based clinical adoption of point-of-care testing (POCT): a systematic narrative review  Crit Rev Clin Lab Sci, 53 (1) (2016), pp.

Research paper

Exploring challenges and policy considerations in point-of-care testing for hospital preparedness ahead of infectious disease emergencies: A qualitative study
==============================================================================================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Oluremilekun Oyefolu, Gigi Kwik Gronvall

Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/)

Open access

Highlights
----------

*   •Point-of-Care Testing (POCT) was beneficial during the COVID-19 pandemic and should be considered as part of pandemic preparedness. 
*   •Some obstacles hindering effective implementation of Point-of-Care Testing in hospitals include the lack of formal training programs for personnel, limited available workforce, and operational difficulties. 
*   •The complexities of federal and state regulatory pathways, particularly in the context of emergency use authorization, present additional challenges to the implementation of POCT, and conflicts between state and federal oversight of POCT. 
*   •There is an emphasis on the need for strategic investments and policy reforms to improve hospital preparedness for future public health emergencies. 

Abstract
--------

### Background

Despite the uncertainty raised by several studies regarding the practicality of Point-of-Care Testing (POCT) in hospital settings, the urgency prompted by the COVID-19 pandemic led many hospitals to invest in these rapid diagnostics. As the COVID-19 pandemic showcased the potential of POCT in emergency situations, an urgent call arises to leverage the successes and lessons learned for the long-term benefit of healthcare systems, particularly in preparation for the next pandemic of viral respiratory nature.

### Methods

We conducted semi-structured interviews with nine emergency care and hospital laboratory stakeholders with expertise and responsibility over POCT operations within hospital systems during the COVID-19 pandemic in Maryland, USA. The grounded theory approach was utilized, and the transcripts were thematically analyzed through an iterative process.

### Results

Three distinct themes were identified as barriers to POCT implementation: Regulatory barriers encompassed inconsistencies between the federal and state regulations and a regulatory structure that lags technological advancements. Staffing and operational barriers comprised a shortage of POCT workforce including nurses and Point of Care coordinators. Economic barriers were linked to the overall cost of integrating new POCT devices into the existing testing menu at emergency departments.

### Conclusion

We conclude with strategies, including policy considerations, to enable and sustain decentralized POCT programs within hospitals. Some of these strategies include expanding the workforce trained in the regulatory procedures required to support POCT; harmonizing state and federal regulations for diagnostic testing, especially for FDA-waived POCT; and sustained funding for POCT development and use for public health preparedness.

*   [Previous article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468045124001081)
*   [Next article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468045124001135)

Keywords
--------

Point-of-care testing

Pandemic preparedness

Emergency departments

COVID-19 legacy

Diagnostic testing

Policy

Introduction
------------

Turnaround time for laboratory tests should be as short as possible. With the creation of more advanced Point-of-Care Testing (POCT) devices that can measure a broader range of analytes, the discovery of biomarkers, the design of microfluidic devices, and improvements in their sensitivity and selectivity, clinical testing may be able to be done closer to where patients get care and outside of the traditional central laboratory setting [[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468045124001093#bib1),[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468045124001093#bib2)]. Even though the technologies that make POCT useful have improved, they are used less than one would expect in clinical settings. Several healthcare stakeholders have yet to agree on the utility of POCT [[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468045124001093#bib1)]. Some think that POCT will only become widely used if the cost savings that could be made can be demonstrated and actualized [[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468045124001093#bib1)]. Others believe that the benefits of POCT (clinical or economic) will only happen once built into a new clinical protocol that acts quickly and effectively on the results [[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468045124001093#bib1)].

The COVID-19 pandemic may have changed how hospital systems view point-of- care testing. The force of the pandemic hit the emergency room harder than any other part of the hospital. It was important to identify COVID-19-infected individuals immediately to treat the patient and stop the risk of transmission. As a result, the emergency department (ED) eventually adopted models that ensured the workflow was better organized and that interventions happened faster. There were more triage options with the use of telemedicine for non-emergent cases [[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468045124001093#bib3)]. The use of rapid tests for SARS- CoV-2 antigen helped emergency rooms run more smoothly [[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468045124001093#bib3)]. New in-vitro diagnostics that make it easy to find and sequence the SARS- CoV-2 virus were also invented [[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468045124001093#bib2),[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468045124001093#bib4),[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468045124001093#bib5)]. Since March 2020, the United States Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has issued Emergency Use Authorization (EUA) to several molecular SARS- CoV-2 diagnostic tests [[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468045124001093#bib2)]. While these molecular diagnostic devices integrate automation into extraction-amplification-detection, have a low and specific limit of detection and have a high concordance with the traditional central laboratory based Reverse Transcriptase-Polymerase Chain Reaction (RT-PCR) methods, they require similar resources to a central laboratory such as stringent conditions, expensive reagents and trained personnel [[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468045124001093#bib2),[[5]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468045124001093#bib5), [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468045124001093#bib6), [[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468045124001093#bib7)]. A large number of automated RT-PCR based diagnostic tests are widely accepted and being used for viral testing in clinical practice [[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468045124001093#bib6),[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468045124001093#bib7)].

This study aims to explore systemic barriers surrounding POCT implementation in hospital systems, particularly the ED, focusing on the automated RT-PCR molecular diagnostic tests during COVID-19 pandemic as a case study. We also share actionable insights for healthcare policymakers or interested stakeholders seeking to leverage POCT technologies for better pandemic preparedness.

Methods
-------

_Study Design_: The grounded theory qualitative approach was adopted as the research strategy. This is particularly suited to topics where little is known. This approach allowed for an in-depth understanding of stakeholders' perspectives on POCT operations in hospital systems, with a specific focus on EDs during the COVID-19 pandemic. The writing and reporting of this research are based on the Standards for Reporting Qualitative Research [[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468045124001093#bib8)].

_Setting:_ This study was conducted in four hospital systems in Maryland, USA, consisting of both community and academic health systems.

_Participants:_ Emergency care and hospital laboratory stakeholders with responsibility and expertise in POCT operations within hospital systems in Maryland, USA were recruited through a combination of purposive sampling. They included pathologists, emergency care physicians, a professor of pathology, a professor of emergency care and a laboratory manager. Among the nine participants, all but one had held leadership positions within their respective departments.

_Data collection:_ Semi structured interviews were the only source of data. Data were collected via Zoom between February 2023 and March 2023. Participant recruitment was achieved through email/fax invitations. Twenty-one interview invitations were sent out to emergency care and hospital laboratory stakeholders. Nine stakeholders responded and agreed to participate in the study. A suitable interview time was scheduled. Each interview lasted approximately 45 min. The interviews were guided by a structured set of questions focusing on five main areas: current POCT testing menu, barriers to decentralized infectious disease testing in hospital systems, opportunities for decentralizing POCT, future trends in POCT, and decentralizing respiratory viral testing to the ED. During each interview, two interviewers independently took extensive notes. After each session, these notes were compared and transcribed before proceeding to the next interview. The whole interviewing cycle took four weeks.

_Data analysis:_ Transcripts were initially analyzed manually to identify significant patterns or concepts. This process was repeated multiple times. Codes and themes were refined and adjusted as new insights emerged. This iterative approach continued until thematic saturation was reached, when no new themes emerged from the data. The identified themes were grouped into broader categories that encapsulated the primary challenges of POCT implementation in hospital systems.

Results
-------

The nine stakeholders interviewed were an associate professor of ED, directors of POCT (_n_=2), emergency medicine physicians (_n_=2), a pathology lab manager, and chairs of ED (_n_=3). For an overview of the themes generated through the analysis, please refer to [Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468045124001093#tbl1).

Table 1. Barriers to implementing a decentralized POCT program in hospital settings.

| Themes | Sub-themes (participant frequency) | Quotations |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Regulatory Issues | Quality assurance and control (6) | “Quality and regulatory requirements are constantly not met by non-lab staff. Although tests were CLIA waived, there are still certain requirements to adhere to in terms of staffing and ability to meet regulatory requirements” (DP-1) “How sensitive is the test to variation in temperature, humidity and other environmental factors? All these must be documented” (DP-1) “Assuming the test is already in existence, what are the barriers to use: what is the status of the test? (FDA approved or not), CLIA waived? What regulatory element is required of the infrastructure, EUA status. A limited regulatory oversight strain would be valuable to the implementation of POCT” (APEM) “Staff competency and verification burden on lab due to the immense turnover of agency nurses. Competency. According to the accreditation standards, the pathology department must verify competency before granting access to POCT instruments. In addition, the competency step involved hundreds of people and there were recordkeeping challenges with that” (DP-2) “Lack of enough experience with POCT personnel and issues with understanding the guidelines of lab medicine in stressful pandemic environment that led to sample labelling errors, and this caused delayed care” (PLM) “Experience is needed for a POCT program to be of benefit during surge periods, for example a pandemic. The trainee needs to test enough to be able to troubleshoot” (DP-2) “The stress of bringing new instrument on-board into their system within few months during the pandemic versus nine months standard in normal times” (PLM) “Quality assurance for the people interpreting the test is more arduous for the ED with large numbers of staff and turnover” (CEM-2) “The regulatory burden was significant for the ED environment which is fast-paced and has moderate turnover of staff” (CEM-2) “Quality control issues were brought up that related to patient throughput” (EMP-2) |
| Inconsistent federal and state regulatory pathways (3) | “Struggling with the frustrating FDA 510 K process and its impact on consumers, pathologists, and vendors.” (PLM) “The FDA is increasingly difficult to work with in the way they regulate in-vitro diagnostics … They are more detailed and granular in intended use (particular patient population and particular setting)” (DP-1) “Rules for RSV POCT only CLIA waived for children aged 7 years and younger. Test has to be done in the central lab for ages 8 years and older ….It is too much throughput to remember and not efficient” (EMP-1) “Struggle to constantly educate POCT personnel on local, state, and federal lab medicine” (PLM) “FDA under CLIA makes the assessment of whether a test receives a waiver, states don’t have to recognize that … None of the molecular COVID –19 testing though waived at the federal level are waived at state level” (DP-1) |
| Impact of EUA cessation (2) | “What happens when the public health emergency goes away? It is a difficult process for labs to turn on CLIA regulation for pathogen testing if expired” (APEM) “Under the state of emergency, molecular devices are treated as CLIA waived. When the EUAs end, manufacturers would need to submit CLIA waiver to FDA. The state of Maryland will default to the normal regulatory path that would lead to a revamping of everything set up in the ED” (DP-2) |
| Innovation outpacing the current regulatory framework (4) | “Inertia based on past experience with quality control issues … there have been technological advances over the last few years that have sorted quality control concerns of devices” (EMP-1) “It is difficult to innovate in the regulatory environment. The FDA does not have the capacity to think about rapid diagnostics using their existing assessment of technology.” (APEM) “Technology is advancing faster than regulatory science, so it is hard for FDA to keep up with all the innovations happening. Regulators are conservative by nature … those doing the regulatory work are frustrated by regulators” (EMP-2) “Some of the current compliance standards do not match the technology of newer generations of POCT devices, which are less error-prone and more reliable” (CEM-2) |
| Staffing | Limited/complicated staffing (6) | “Does the ED have the capacity to staff lab 24/7? … EDs are also understaffed and often not staffed 24 h either” (APEM) “The ED had to pull clinical staff from circulation to do non-clinical testing work” (DP-1) “POCT lab needs to be staffed 24 h/day which is challenging. If unstaffed at any time (at night), the order is redirected to the central lab, thereby slowing things down. Nobody knows where to inquire about updates on pending order results leading to confusion and delay” (CEM-1) “… ED environment which is fast paced and has moderate turnover of staff” (CEM-2) “EDs are understaffed … If you have a small number of people that have been trained, they are not going to be there all the time, that makes for complicated staffing” (EMP-2) “POC coordinator is a difficult position to hire because there is currently no specific training pathway for POCT … The job description of a POC coordinator is extensive and bureaucratic and is determined by the CAP or the joint Commission accrediting body” (DP-2) “No designated leadership for POC … Pathologists are not specifically trained in POC but learn on the job” (DP-2) Agreed to build a satellite lab into the ED that would be staffed 12 h by one technician who would be responsible for all POCT … It failed within a month because there were troubles staffing … it was expensive to sustain salary of POCT lab staff even though the tests were low cost” (EMP-2) |
| Operational issues | Limited capacity due to nature of ED (4) • Space constraint • Storage capacity • Data connectivity interface | “Is the lab taking up space that can otherwise be used for patient care” (APEM) “It can be a hard time implementing POCT. Beds are always in high demand. Patients waiting 4–6/2-3 h for results occupy beds” (APEM) “POCT supplies need to be stored in the ED versus central location otherwise the benefit of saved time would be lost” (CEM-2) “Do you have the space and capacity to do the testing? Some of it has to do with the machines that have hoods and proper ventilation” (CEM-3) “Do you need wireless data connectivity … Is there a data interface between the hospital and lab situation?” (DP-1) |
| Cost consideration | Cost- benefit analysis (8) | “The value proposition case before adding a new POCT to the menu: consider what the value of each test is to the patient, provider, and bill coder person against the regulatory environment experience” (APEM) “There are hidden costs of reagents, unreimbursed efforts for maintaining regulatory quality requirement, oversight from the department of pathology, cost of people pulled away from clinical care and having to perform the test, cost of testing personnel” (DP-1) “For a high volume test, 10,000 patients per year, the cost differential goes up very quickly versus if it were a low volume test” (DP-1) “For technological advances to be incorporated in clinical settings, there needs to be a gap that it fills commercially” (DP-1) “It costs more to run tests in an ED lab” (CEM-2) “POCT is more expensive than lab run tests” (DP-2) “If the institution is willing to make the lab financially whole for the cost of staffing the ED lab, ED wait times may be reduced” (EMP-2) “Cost of maintenance of machines may be duplicative to the main lab … there would be staffing costs for a separate area … CLIA type of training and CLIA approval” (CEM-3) “There needs to be a balance of saved time, accuracy and resources (availability of money for staffing, training, equipment) … In the case of COVID-19 flu, it makes a difference because it makes easy for isolation and making decisions about other further intervention (CT scans and laboratory studies)” (CEM-1) “There are hidden costs of reagents, unreimbursed efforts for maintaining regulatory quality requirement, oversight from the department of pathology, cost of people pulled away from clinical care and having to perform the test (if test takes 15 mins, 25% of every hour is taken from other direct patient care or administrative duty)” (DP-2) “It costs more to run tests in an ED lab” (DP-1) “… Agreed to build a satellite lab into ED that would be staffed 12 h by one technician responsible for all POCT testing and simple tests like hematocrit, CBC. It failed within a month because it was expensive to sustain salary of POCT lab staff even though test were low cost tests” (EMP-1) “The cost of maintenance of machines may be duplicative to the main lab and staffing costs for a separate area and CLIA type of training and approval” (CEM-3) “There needs to a balance of saved time versus accuracy versus resources (availability of money for staffing, training, equipment). In the case of COVID-19 flu, it makes a difference because it makes easy for isolation and making decisions about other further intervention (CT scans and laboratory studies)” (CEM-1) |
| Organizational politics and dynamics | Decision making power (5) | “The ED and pathology compete with each other for their budgets” (EMP-1) “Pathology departments typically have the final say on what POCT can be implemented.” (CEM-3) “The decision of what POCT menu is housed in the ED was made by the lab director.” (EMP-2) “If test is done POC in ED, it is hard for the lab to charge the test.” (EMP-2) “If there is something ED likes they talk to pathology and express an opinion on a case by case basis” (CEM-1) “All testing needs has to be run through with pathology because nothing can be implemented without their approval because they are the holder of the CLIA certificate from the regulatory standpoint” (DP-2) |

Note: APEM=Associate professor of emergency medicine, DP=Deputy director of POCT, CEM= Chair of emergency medicine, ED=Emergency department, POCT= Point-of-Care Testing, POC=Point of Care, CBC=complete blood count, lab=laboratory, NIAID= National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases, FDA= US Food and Drug Administration, CLIA= Clinical Laboratory Improvement Amendments, EUA=Emergency Use Authorization, CAP= The College of American Pathologists' Accreditation Program, EMR=electronic medical record, QA=quality assurance.

Discussion
----------

### Challenges of implementing a decentralized POCT program in hospital settings

#### Regulatory issues

##### Quality control and assurance issues

Implementing POCT in the ED has faced various quality control issues, including difficulty complying with manufacturer’s specifications and storage requirements, staff training and competency validation, and regulatory compliance with laboratory accreditation and regulatory standards. While adopting testing policies that allow only a small pool of staff to operate moderately complex POCT devices may alleviate some concerns about test proficiency, staff training and competency validation still place a substantial verification burden on laboratory staff, especially amidst the recent staff shortages and turnover.

The College of American Pathologists' Accreditation Program (CAP) or Joint Commission Accreditation checklist and standards required for POCT program operations were described as extensive, bureaucratic, and often inflexible. The associated burden of rigorous reporting and documentation requirements is often unsustainable in the fast-paced ED with a moderate staff turnover. One participant noted that current compliance standards don’t align with the newer POCT technologies that are more reliable. Clinical Laboratory Improvement Amendment (CLIA) certificates require Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) approved laboratory accreditation status, such as the CAP and the Joint Commission [[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468045124001093#bib9)]**.**

##### Inconsistent federal and state regulatory pathways

The regulatory pathway from federal to state approval of POCT devices is a complex process and poses a barrier to the development and clinical uptake of POCT. State CLIA agencies provide oversight over the use of laboratory-developed tests within their states. After reviewing the analytical performance of the new test system, the state agency may approve a new test system for use within the specific laboratory environment before its approval or clearance by the FDA [[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468045124001093#bib10)]. The policies implemented by the FDA during the COVID-19 pandemic allowed states and territories to authorize laboratories within their jurisdiction to develop and conduct COVID-19 testing without needing an EUA [[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468045124001093#bib11)]. The policy aimed to increase access to COVID-19 testing in the early phases of the pandemic (March 2020) [[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468045124001093#bib11)]. However, this policy translated to granting state agencies the ultimate power to decide which COVID-19 diagnostic test could be performed within the state based on the outcome of their review of analytical performance. As a result, there were situations where COVID-19 testing devices, though CLIA waived and approved at the federal level, were not approved at the state level and thus could not be used as CLIA-waived products within the state.

##### Impact of EUA cessation

It is uncertain what impact the cessation of the EUA would have on a waived designation of POCT tests. State agencies do not have to recognize the FDA’s transition pathway for POCT from EUA to an approved waived status. They are likely to default to the standard regulatory path, which would result in the withdrawal of the waiver status of the POCT product and lead to revamping the POCT menu set up in the ED at the end of the public health emergency. Retroactive withdrawals of POCT devices from the POCT ED menu can destabilize workflow in the ED.

##### Innovation outpacing the current regulatory framework

Technology is advancing faster than regulatory science, so it is hard for regulatory agencies to keep up with recent innovations. However, this challenge applies to general diagnostics, vaccines, and medications. The COVID-19 pandemic has shown that the FDA does not currently have adequate capacity to assess the high volume of innovations in diagnostics technology in a timely fashion during a public health emergency [[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468045124001093#bib11)]. The government must prioritize efforts to improve the regulatory framework for evalua

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

enriched_points.expanded_content: Many individuals in low- and middle-income countries face significant geographical barriers, making access to point-of-care diagnostics challenging, as they are often located far from healthcare facilities.
Mobility issues, including disabilities or lack of transportation, hinder patients' ability to visit testing sites, necessitating the development of portable point-of-care diagnostic solutions.
The rising demand for home testing reflects the need for convenient, accessible diagnostics that empower patients to manage their health without visiting medical facilities.
